constraints: Frontend and backend built in a single Next.js 16.x TypeScript codebase compatible with Node v24.11.0/npm 11.6.1
SQLite persistence via better-sqlite3 with automatic DB creation on first run
Exactly 3 playable puzzles with data-driven definitions and lock/unlock progression
Block workspace must have left library/right code area with fixed `On Start` root, warning for disconnected blocks, categories for movement/actions/control/logic/sensing
Run controls include prominent Play/Reset/speed toggle; workspace minimizes during execution
Failure feedback must display `Oops!` plus hint for specific failure reasons (target not reached, wrong item, wrong order, obstacle collision, missing condition)
Scene assets must use provided PNGs placed per layout reference (character, place tiles, obstacle, goal) with WebGL-based polished cartoon visuals in pink/violet palette
Drag-and-drop block editor (custom or Blockly) with substantial custom theming; `Show Code` toggle for textual view
Telemetry records all gameplay/UI events including movement steps and block execution, sent via HTTP to backend
Analytics UI must read SQLite data, provide dashboard, puzzle detail, attempt history, event stream, and movement replay
success_criteria: Three puzzles teaching sequencing, loops, conditionals with progressive difficulty
Workspace layout and controls per spec, including `On Start` root and speed toggle
Running programs animates scene; incorrect runs show `Oops!` + hint
Telemetry writes `attempts`,`events`,`movements` rows for completed runs with movement
SQLite DB auto-initializes and stores required tables
Analytics pages can replay runs using stored movement data
Game assets arranged per provided layout reference with original characters and polished styling
expected_outputs: Next.js 16 TypeScript application source covering frontend UI, backend API routes, and shared logic
Puzzle data definitions enabling future additions without core code changes
Telemetry ingestion APIs (`POST /api/session/start`, `/end`, `/api/events/batch`) and analytics `GET /api/analytics/*` endpoints
Game scenes using provided assets with puzzle progression, execution engine, failure feedback, and block workspace
Analytics dashboard and replay tooling consuming stored telemetry
stack_requirements: Next.js 16.x with TypeScript for both frontend and backend features
WebGL-based 2D rendering layer supporting smooth sprite animation
Drag-and-drop block editor (Blockly or custom) with custom styling
Optional text-to-speech for goal text; accessibility-friendly controls
validation_expectations: Build/start scripts succeed for Next.js app
Database initialization runs automatically and creates required tables
Manual or automated checks confirm telemetry rows written for runs and movement steps
Runtime verification that analytics endpoints read stored data and replay movement paths
Functional testing ensuring three puzzles playable with success/failure handling and progression
UI verification for block workspace layout, run controls, and failure messaging
repo_constraints: Use assets from `public/assets/backgrounds/` and `public/assets/sprites/`; follow `design/layout_refs/layout.png` for initial scene composition
Treat provided layout reference as canonical for asset placement; extrapolate layout logic to later levels
No proprietary third-party characters or branding; maintain original visual identity
Maintain beginner-friendly, high-contrast pink/violet cartoon style across game and analytics views
missing_information: Exact tile/grid sizing and coordinates for puzzles beyond guidance from layout reference
Specific narrative/story text, goal descriptions, and hints for each puzzle
Precise animation timing, speed toggle values, and loop safety cap thresholds
Detailed telemetry payload schemas and buffering strategy
Exact analytics dashboard layout and metrics beyond general requirements
assumptions: Future puzzles will reuse the defined data format for extensibility
Layout reference provides sufficient guidance to infer positions for all scenes; remaining levels mimic spacing logic
Goal text for text-to-speech can be derived from puzzle definitions
WebGL rendering can be implemented via a compatible library (e.g., PixiJS) within Next.js without violating constraints
